answer:
Manu's Chapter 1 (Manusmṛti, unknown-manusmrti-347b76, ch. 1) grounds varna cosmogonically: only after 86 verses narrating the Self-existent One's creation of the universe — waters, elements, mind, time, the Vedas, the yugas — does varna appear, and when it does (Mn_1.31), the four orders literally emanate from the divine body: Brahmaṇa from the mouth, Kṣatriya from the arms, Vaiśya from the thighs, Śūdra from the feet. Their duties (Mn_1.87–1.91) and the Brahmaṇa's supremacy (Mn_1.92–1.101) are then defended by the same cosmogonic logic: born from the highest limb, purest because the mouth is the purest part of the cosmic person. This is the Puruṣa-sūkta structure: varna is written into the fabric of creation itself and therefore eternal.

Yājñavalkya's Chapter 1 (Yājñavalkya Smṛti, unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6, ch. 1) opens on entirely different ground. The sages ask about the dharma of the varṇas and āśramas, and Yājñavalkya responds by enumerating the fourteen sources of dharmic knowledge — Purāṇas, Nyāya, Mīmāṃsā, Dharmaśāstra, and the Vedas (Yj_1.3) — then listing eighteen earlier Smṛtikāras as authoritative, Manu among them (Yj_1.4–1.5). Dharma is defined epistemologically: revelation, tradition, conduct of the virtuous, pleasing to one's own self, and right resolve (Yj_1.7). Only at Yj_1.10 does varna appear: 'Brāhmaṇa, Kṣatriya, Vaiśya, and Śūdra are the varṇas; the first three are the twice-born' — a flat definitional statement with no cosmic derivation. The text moves immediately into the saṃskāra system and initiation rules differentiated by varna.

Has Yājñavalkya genuinely shifted the framework, or simply stripped the cosmogonic prelude? The shift is genuine but partial. The content of varna (the four orders, their hierarchy, their rites) is substantially retained. What changes is the justificatory architecture: Manu requires creation-myth authority — varna is valid because the cosmic person's body is why it exists. Yājñavalkya requires textual-traditional authority — varna is valid because a chain of eighteen Smṛtikāras (including Manu) and the Vedas declare it. Yājñavalkya also introduces 'the vision of the Self through yoga' as the highest dharma (Yj_1.8), a register absent from Manu's chapter 1, which suggests a theological reorientation away from sacrificial-cosmogonic dharma toward internalized realization. So Yājñavalkya has not merely abbreviated Manu; he has relocated the ground of varna's authority from ontology (it is because the cosmic person made it so) to epistemology and tradition (it is because the śāstras and ācāryas teach it). That is a genuine, if non-revolutionary, framework shift.

tool_calls.result_summary: Opening confirmed: sages ask about varna/āśrama dharma (Yj_1.1); Yājñavalkya responds with 14 sources of dharma (Yj_1.3), 18 Smṛtikāra authorities (Yj_1.4–1.5), epistemological definition of dharma (Yj_1.6–1.9), then flat varna definition without cosmogony (Yj_1.10), immediately followed by saṃskāra/initiation procedures
